name: Latency SLO Guard
description: Monitors service response time against a defined SLO target. Triggers an alert or blocks deployment when the p99 latency exceeds the threshold. Ideal for user-facing APIs where response time directly impacts experience.

nodes.label: Latency SLO Guard
nodes.description: Ensures p99 response time stays within the defined SLO target. Measures end-to-end latency from client request to response.

name: Coupling Guard
description: Measures component fan-out (number of outgoing dependencies) and flags modules that exceed the threshold. High fan-out indicates tight coupling and fragile change propagation.

nodes.label: Coupling Guard
nodes.description: Checks that no component has more than N outgoing dependencies. Prevents tightly-coupled designs that resist change.

name: Deployment Frequency
description: Tracks how often the team deploys to production. One of the four DORA metrics. Low deployment frequency often correlates with large, risky batch releases.

nodes.label: Deployment Frequency
nodes.description: Measures deployments per week/month. Target: on-demand or at least weekly for high-performing teams.

name: Security Dependency Scan
description: Scans all runtime and build dependencies for known CVEs before deployment. Blocks releases containing critical or high-severity vulnerabilities. Essential for supply-chain security.

nodes.label: Security Dependency Scan
nodes.description: Scans dependencies for CVEs. Fails pipeline on critical/high severity findings. Uses tools like Snyk, Trivy or npm audit.

name: API Contract Compliance
description: Validates that API implementations conform to their OpenAPI/AsyncAPI specifications. Catches breaking changes before they reach consumers. Critical for microservice architectures.

nodes.label: API Contract Compliance
nodes.description: Validates implementation against OpenAPI/AsyncAPI spec. Detects breaking changes (removed fields, changed types, dropped endpoints).

name: Data Residency Check
description: Continuously verifies that personal data is stored and processed only in approved geographic regions. Required for GDPR, data sovereignty laws, and contractual obligations.

nodes.label: Data Residency Check
nodes.description: Verifies that PII is stored only in approved regions (e.g. EU-only for GDPR). Checks cloud resource tags, database locations, and CDN configs.
nodes.threshold: All PII stored in approved regions only

name: Error Budget Burn Rate
description: Tracks the consumption rate of the error budget (derived from SLO). When the burn rate exceeds thresholds, it triggers alerts or freezes feature releases until reliability improves. Key SRE practice.

nodes.label: Error Budget Burn Rate
nodes.description: Monitors error budget consumption. Alert at 50% consumed in first quarter of window. Feature freeze at 80% consumed.

name: Test Coverage Gate
description: Enforces a minimum code coverage percentage on every PR/deployment. Prevents untested code from reaching production. Best combined with mutation testing for quality, not just quantity.

nodes.label: Test Coverage Gate
nodes.description: Blocks deployment when line/branch coverage falls below the threshold. Ratchets up: new code must meet or exceed current coverage.
nodes.threshold: ≥ 80% line coverage, no decrease on diff

name: Lead Time for Changes
description: Measures the time from code commit to running in production. One of the four DORA metrics. Shorter lead times indicate a healthier CI/CD pipeline and faster feedback loops.

nodes.label: Lead Time for Changes
nodes.description: Tracks time from commit to production deploy. Target: less than one day for elite performers (DORA).

name: Mean Time to Recovery
description: Measures how long it takes to restore service after a production incident. One of the four DORA metrics. Shorter MTTR indicates better incident response and system resilience.

nodes.label: Mean Time to Recovery
nodes.description: Tracks time from incident detection to service restoration. Target: less than one hour for elite performers (DORA).

name: System Availability
description: Ensures the system meets its availability target. Ubiquitous requirement — applies at all times without preconditions.

nodes.label: System Availability
nodes.action: be available 99.9% of the time, measured monthly
nodes.rationale: Business SLA requires three-nines availability to meet customer contracts and avoid penalty clauses.

name: Auto-Scaling Under Load
description: Event-driven requirement that triggers horizontal scaling when load exceeds a threshold.

nodes.label: Auto-Scaling Under Load
nodes.trigger: incoming request rate exceeds 1000 requests per second
nodes.action: automatically scale out compute instances within 60 seconds to maintain response time below 500ms
nodes.rationale: Traffic spikes during promotions and seasonal events must be handled without manual intervention.

name: API Authentication
description: All API requests must be authenticated. Ubiquitous security requirement with no preconditions.

nodes.label: API Authentication
nodes.action: authenticate all API requests using OAuth 2.0 bearer tokens before processing
nodes.rationale: Unauthenticated access exposes sensitive data and operations to unauthorized parties.

name: Response Time Under Load
description: State-driven requirement: while the system is under normal operating conditions, API responses must complete within a target time.

nodes.label: Response Time Under Load
nodes.precondition: the system is operating under normal load (< 80% CPU, < 70% memory)
nodes.action: respond to any API request within 500ms at the 95th percentile
nodes.rationale: Perceived responsiveness directly impacts user satisfaction and conversion rates.

name: Data Retention Policy
description: Unwanted-behaviour requirement: prevents unbounded data growth by archiving old data.

nodes.label: Data Retention Policy
nodes.unwanted_condition: transactional data is older than 90 days
nodes.action: archive the data to cold storage and remove it from the primary database
nodes.rationale: Unbounded data growth degrades query performance and increases storage costs.

name: GDPR Data Encryption
description: Optional feature requirement: when handling EU user data, encryption at rest is mandatory.

nodes.label: GDPR Data Encryption
nodes.feature: EU user personal data is stored or processed
nodes.action: encrypt all personal data at rest using AES-256 and in transit using TLS 1.2+
nodes.rationale: GDPR Article 32 requires appropriate technical measures to protect personal data.

name: Disaster Recovery Failover
description: Event-driven requirement: when the primary region fails, the system must failover to a secondary region within a defined time window.

nodes.label: Disaster Recovery Failover
nodes.trigger: the primary region becomes unavailable for more than 30 seconds
nodes.action: automatically failover to the secondary region with an RTO of 5 minutes and RPO of 1 minute
nodes.rationale: Business continuity requires rapid recovery from regional outages to minimize revenue loss.

name: Audit Logging
description: Ubiquitous requirement: all state-changing operations must be logged for audit and compliance purposes.

nodes.label: Audit Logging
nodes.action: log all state-changing operations with timestamp, actor identity, action type, affected resource, and before/after values
nodes.rationale: Regulatory compliance (SOX, PCI-DSS) and security forensics require a complete, immutable audit trail.

name: API Rate Limiting
description: Complex requirement combining state and event conditions to protect the system from abuse.

nodes.label: API Rate Limiting
nodes.precondition: the API gateway is operating normally
nodes.trigger: a single client exceeds 100 requests per minute
nodes.action: reject excess requests with HTTP 429 and include a Retry-After header
nodes.rationale: Prevents individual clients from monopolizing resources and degrading service for others.

name: WCAG Accessibility
description: Ubiquitous requirement ensuring the UI meets accessibility standards for all users.

nodes.label: WCAG Accessibility
nodes.action: conform to WCAG 2.1 Level AA for all user-facing pages and interactive components
nodes.rationale: Inclusive design ensures the product is usable by people with disabilities and meets legal requirements in many jurisdictions.

name: Technology Stack Selection
description: Template ADR for selecting the primary technology stack (language, framework, runtime). Captures the decision context, constraints, and trade-offs of choosing a tech stack.

nodes.label: ADR: Technology Stack Selection
nodes.context: We need to select a primary technology stack for the new service / module. Key factors: team expertise, ecosystem maturity, performance characteristics, hiring market, and long-term maintainability.
nodes.decision: We will use [LANGUAGE/FRAMEWORK] because [RATIONALE]. This choice balances team familiarity, ecosystem support, and performance requirements.
nodes.consequences:
• Team members unfamiliar with the stack will need onboarding (estimated 2-3 weeks)
• We gain access to [ECOSYSTEM BENEFIT]
• Lock-in: migrating away would require [EFFORT ESTIMATE]
nodes.alternatives:
1. [Alternative A] — rejected because [reason]
2. [Alternative B] — rejected because [reason]
3. [Alternative C] — viable but less preferred because [reason]

name: Data Store Selection
description: Template ADR for choosing the primary data store (relational, document, key-value, graph, etc.). Considers consistency, scalability, query patterns, and operational complexity.

nodes.label: ADR: Data Store Selection
nodes.context: The service needs a persistent data store. Key considerations: data model complexity (relational vs document), consistency requirements (strong vs eventual), expected query patterns (OLTP vs OLAP), scalability needs, and operational team capabilities.
nodes.decision: We will use [DATABASE] as the primary data store because [RATIONALE]. It provides the right balance of consistency, query flexibility, and operational simplicity for our use case.
nodes.consequences:
• Schema management via [TOOL/APPROACH]
• Backup and DR strategy: [APPROACH]
• Vendor lock-in risk: [LOW/MEDIUM/HIGH]
• Estimated operational cost: [ESTIMATE]
nodes.alternatives:
1. [Alternative DB A] — rejected: [reason]
2. [Alternative DB B] — rejected: [reason]
3. Multi-model approach — rejected: operational complexity too high at current team size

name: Authentication Strategy
description: Template ADR for choosing the authentication mechanism (JWT, session-based, OAuth 2.0, OIDC, mTLS). Covers security, scalability, and user experience trade-offs.

nodes.label: ADR: Authentication Strategy
nodes.context: We need to authenticate users and services. Requirements: support for SSO, token-based stateless auth for APIs, refresh token rotation, and compatibility with our frontend framework. Must support both human users and machine-to-machine (M2M) scenarios.
nodes.decision: We will use [OAUTH 2.0 / JWT / SESSION-BASED / mTLS] because [RATIONALE]. Human users authenticate via [FLOW], machine clients via [FLOW].
nodes.consequences:
• Token storage: [APPROACH]
• Token lifetime and refresh strategy: [DETAILS]
• Revocation mechanism: [APPROACH]
• Impact on latency: [ESTIMATE]

name: Integration Pattern
description: Template ADR for choosing between synchronous (REST/gRPC) and asynchronous (event-driven/messaging) integration between services. Key architectural decision for distributed systems.

nodes.label: ADR: Integration Pattern
nodes.context: Services need to communicate. We must decide between synchronous request-response (REST, gRPC) and asynchronous messaging (events, commands via message broker). Factors: latency requirements, coupling tolerance, reliability needs, ordering guarantees, and team familiarity.
nodes.decision: We will use [PATTERN] as the default integration style because [RATIONALE]. Exceptions: [LIST EXCEPTIONS AND WHY].
nodes.consequences:
• Debugging and tracing: [APPROACH — correlation IDs, distributed tracing]
• Error handling: [APPROACH — retries, DLQ, circuit breakers]
• Data consistency: [EVENTUAL vs STRONG, saga pattern if needed]
• Infrastructure: [BROKER/GATEWAY needed]
nodes.alternatives:
1. Pure sync (REST) — rejected: creates runtime coupling chains
2. Pure async — rejected: adds complexity for simple queries
3. Hybrid (proposed) — sync for queries, async for commands/events

name: Deployment Strategy
description: Template ADR for choosing the deployment strategy (blue-green, canary, rolling, recreate). Balances risk, rollback speed, and infrastructure cost.

nodes.label: ADR: Deployment Strategy
nodes.context: We need a reliable deployment strategy that minimizes downtime and risk. Considerations: zero-downtime requirement, rollback speed, infrastructure cost, database migration compatibility, and observability during rollout.
nodes.decision: We will use [BLUE-GREEN / CANARY / ROLLING] deployments because [RATIONALE]. Rollback is achieved by [MECHANISM].
nodes.consequences:
• Infrastructure cost: [ESTIMATE — e.g. 2x for blue-green]
• Rollback time: [ESTIMATE]
• Database migration approach: [APPROACH — backwards-compatible migrations]
• Monitoring during rollout: [METRICS TO WATCH]
nodes.alternatives:
1. Blue-green — instant rollback but 2x infrastructure cost
2. Canary — gradual rollout, requires good observability
3. Rolling — least infrastructure overhead but slower rollback
4. Recreate — simplest but causes downtime

name: Observability Strategy
description: Template ADR for choosing the observability stack and approach (logs, metrics, traces). Covers the three pillars of observability and their tooling.

nodes.label: ADR: Observability Strategy
nodes.context: We need a comprehensive observability strategy covering the three pillars: structured logging, metrics, and distributed tracing. The strategy must support debugging production issues, measuring SLOs, and understanding system behaviour across service boundaries.
nodes.decision: We will use [TOOLING STACK] implementing OpenTelemetry for instrumentation. Logs: [TOOL]. Metrics: [TOOL]. Traces: [TOOL]. Dashboards: [TOOL].
nodes.consequences:
• All services must emit structured logs in JSON format
• Correlation IDs propagated via [HEADER] across all service calls
• SLO dashboards created for each critical user journey
• Estimated additional infrastructure cost: [ESTIMATE]
nodes.alternatives:
1. [Alternative stack A] — rejected: [reason]
2. Vendor-locked solution — rejected: portability concern
3. Build custom — rejected: undifferentiated heavy lifting

name: Microservices Architecture
description: A microservices decomposition with an API Gateway, two backend services, shared database per service, and message queue for async communication. Each service owns its data store.

nodes.label: API Gateway
nodes.description: Entry point for all client requests. Routes, authenticates, and rate-limits incoming traffic.

nodes.label: Service A
nodes.description: Handles domain capability A. Owns its data store and exposes APIs consumed by the gateway.

nodes.label: Service A Database
nodes.description: Private data store for Service A. Not shared with other services.

nodes.label: Service B
nodes.description: Handles domain capability B. Communicates with Service A via events.

nodes.label: Service B Database
nodes.description: Private data store for Service B.

nodes.label: Message Broker
nodes.description: Async event bus for inter-service communication. Decouples services temporally.

name: Event-Driven Architecture
description: An event-driven system where producers emit domain events to a central event bus, and multiple consumers react independently. Enables loose coupling and scalable processing.

nodes.label: Event Producer
nodes.description: Generates domain events when state changes occur. Events are immutable facts about what happened.

nodes.label: Event Bus
nodes.description: Central event backbone. Persists events and delivers them to subscribed consumers. Supports replay and partitioning.

nodes.label: Consumer: Projections
nodes.description: Builds read-optimized views (CQRS query side) by consuming domain events.

nodes.label: Consumer: Notifications
nodes.description: Sends notifications (email, push, SMS) in response to domain events.

name: CQRS + Event Sourcing
description: Separates command (write) and query (read) models. Commands produce events stored in an event store; projections build read-optimized views from those events.

nodes.label: Command Handler
nodes.description: Validates and processes commands. Produces domain events. Enforces business invariants.

nodes.label: Event Store
nodes.description: Append-only log of all domain events. The single source of truth. Supports event replay for rebuilding state.

nodes.label: Projection Engine
nodes.description: Consumes events and builds read-optimized views. Runs asynchronously and can be rebuilt from scratch.

nodes.label: Read Store
nodes.description: Denormalized read model optimized for query patterns. Updated by projections.

nodes.label: Query Handler
nodes.description: Serves read requests from the denormalized read store. No business logic, no writes.

name: API Gateway Pattern
description: Centralizes cross-cutting concerns (auth, rate limiting, routing, TLS termination) in a single entry point. Backend services focus on business logic without repeating infrastructure concerns.

nodes.label: API Consumer
nodes.description: External client (web app, mobile app, third-party integration) that consumes the public API.

nodes.label: API Gateway
nodes.description: Edge proxy handling: TLS termination, authentication, rate limiting, request routing, response caching, and API versioning.

nodes.label: User Service
nodes.description: Manages user accounts, profiles, and preferences.

nodes.label: Order Service
nodes.description: Handles order creation, processing, and fulfilment.

name: Strangler Fig Migration
description: Incrementally migrates a monolith to a new architecture by routing traffic through a facade. New features go to the new system; legacy features are migrated one by one until the monolith can be decommissioned.

nodes.label: Strangler Facade
nodes.description: Routes requests to either the legacy monolith or the new services based on feature flags / URL patterns. Transparent to consumers.

nodes.label: Legacy Monolith
nodes.description: The existing system being migrated. Features are removed from here as they're rebuilt in the new services.

nodes.label: New Service
nodes.description: Modern replacement for a subset of monolith functionality. Built with the target architecture.

nodes.label: New Service Database
nodes.description: Dedicated data store for the new service. Data migrated from the monolith's shared database.

name: Hexagonal Architecture (Ports & Adapters)
description: Isolates business logic from infrastructure via ports (interfaces) and adapters (implementations). The domain core has no dependencies on frameworks, databases, or external services.

nodes.label: Domain Core
nodes.description: Pure business logic and domain rules. No infrastructure dependencies. Defines ports (interfaces) that adapters implement.

nodes.label: Application Service
nodes.description: Orchestrates use cases by calling domain objects and port interfaces. Thin layer — no business rules here.

nodes.label: REST Adapter (Driving)
nodes.description: Inbound adapter: translates HTTP requests into application service calls. Implements the driving port.

nodes.label: Database Adapter (Driven)
nodes.description: Outbound adapter: implements the repository port to persist domain objects in a database.

nodes.label: External API Adapter (Driven)
nodes.description: Outbound adapter: implements a port to call external services (payment, email, etc.).
